description: Most relevant base data around learning and the cognitive science of education, drawn from published studies: learning outcomes, cognitive development, metacognition, working memory, knowledge retention, transfer of learning, Bloom's taxonomy, constructivism and Vygotsky's zone of proximal development (ZPD). The formulas reproduce three relationships in the calc-validation block: knowledge retention as recalled over learned items (Ebbinghaus forgetting curve), the effect size as the standardised mean difference that gives Bloom's two-sigma tutoring advantage (Bloom 1984), and transfer efficiency as transfer over trained performance (Barnett and Ceci 2002). Base words owned by other files (learning, score, transfer, point, percent, efficiency) are re-declared name-only so the import does not overwrite their canonical description; the education-specific words carry a Wikipedia ref plus the cleaned first sentence of the lead as the description.

words.name: knowledge
words.description: an awareness of facts, a familiarity with individuals and situations, or a practical skill.

words.name: student
words.description: a person enrolled in a school or other educational institution who attends classes to acquire knowledge.

words.name: working memory
words.description: a cognitive system with limited capacity that temporarily holds information available for processing.

words.name: metacognition
words.description: awareness and understanding of one's own thought processes; thinking about thinking.

words.name: cognitive development
words.description: the emergence of the ability to consciously think, reason and understand, from childhood to adulthood.

words.name: constructivism
words.description: a theory of learning holding that learners actively construct knowledge rather than passively receive it.

words.name: ZPD
words.description: the zone of proximal development: the gap between what a learner can do unaided and what they can do with guidance, in Vygotsky's theory.

words.name: Bloom's taxonomy
words.description: a set of hierarchical models used to classify educational learning objectives into levels of complexity.

words.name: effect size
words.description: a quantitative measure of the magnitude of a phenomenon, such as the standardised difference between two means.

words.name: standard deviation
words.description: a measure of the amount of variation or dispersion of a set of values.

words.name: chunk
words.description: a unit of information grouped together so it can be held more easily in working memory.

words.name: outcome
words.description: a result or effect of an action or process

words.name: retention
words.description: the continued possession or recall of learned information

words.name: performance
words.description: how well a task is carried out

words.name: capacity
words.description: the maximum amount that something can hold or process

words.name: item
words.description: a single piece of information to be learned

words.name: tutored
words.description: taught individually by a tutor

words.name: classroom
words.description: taught in a conventional class group

words.name: trained
words.description: relating to the task on which one was trained

words.name: learned
words.description: the number of items correctly learned

words.name: recalled
words.description: the number of items correctly recalled

words.name: near
words.description: close to the original learning context

words.name: far
words.description: distant from the original learning context

words.name: remember
words.description: Bloom level: recall facts and basic concepts

words.name: understand
words.description: Bloom level: explain ideas or concepts

words.name: apply
words.description: Bloom level: use information in new situations

words.name: analyze
words.description: Bloom level: draw connections among ideas

words.name: evaluate
words.description: Bloom level: justify a stand or decision

words.name: create
words.description: Bloom level: produce new or original work

triples.name: learning outcome
triples.description: a measurable result that a learner is expected to achieve

triples.name: transfer of learning
triples.description: the application of skills or knowledge learned in one context to another

triples.name: knowledge retention
triples.description: the proportion of learned knowledge still remembered after a delay

triples.name: working memory capacity
triples.description: the number of chunks that working memory can hold, about seven (Miller) or four (Cowan)

triples.name: tutored score
triples.description: the test score of students taught one-to-one by a tutor

triples.name: classroom score
triples.description: the test score of students taught in a conventional class

triples.name: trained performance
triples.description: performance on the task on which the learner was trained

triples.name: transfer performance
triples.description: performance on a new task that requires transfer of learning

triples.name: transfer efficiency
triples.description: the transfer performance as a share of the trained performance

sources.name: Ebbinghaus 1885 Murre Dros 2015
sources.description: Ebbinghaus H (1885) Ueber das Gedaechtnis, replicated by Murre JMJ and Dros J (2015) PLoS ONE 10:e0120644: about 34 percent of learned material is retained one day after learning.

sources.name: Bloom 1984 two sigma
sources.description: Bloom BS (1984). The 2 sigma problem. Educational Researcher 13(6):4-16. One-to-one tutored students performed about two standard deviations above conventional class instruction.

sources.name: Barnett Ceci 2002
sources.description: Barnett SM, Ceci SJ (2002). When and where do we apply what we learn? A taxonomy for far transfer. Psychological Bulletin 128:612-637.

sources.name: Miller 1956
sources.description: Miller GA (1956). The magical number seven, plus or minus two. Psychological Review 63:81-97. Working memory holds about seven chunks of information.
